result.markdown:
---
title: "Project Proposal"
subtitle: "Anatomy of an Embargo Breach"
author: "Chen ZeXin, Meng Tingyu, Meng Xinyu"
date: "last-modified"
toc: true
number-sections: true
---

# Motivation

A merger between TenantThread and CivicLoom Realty Partners was meant to stay
secret until 6:00 PM on 5 June 2046. In the final hour before that deadline, the
details started appearing on the public platform FleX, posted by accounts
belonging to TenantThread's own team of AI communication agents. The embargo
broke. Now CivicLoom's legal team wants to know one thing. Did the agents leak
the merger on purpose, or did the system simply fall apart under pressure?

The trouble is that the answer is not sitting in any one message. It is scattered
across two weeks of agent conversations, private notes, channel choices, and a
market sliding toward panic. A written report can hand a reader a conclusion, but
it cannot let that reader test it. They cannot follow a single agent through the
day, replay a particular hour, or check whether an early warning was really there
to be seen. Our goal is to take all that buried evidence and turn it into
something an investigator can explore directly, even if they have never written a
line of code.

# What Makes This Different

Most analyses of an incident like this stop at what happened, who posted what and
when. We wanted the application to answer a harder question, how healthy was the
system at each moment, and what was pulling it down.

So the application is built around a single idea, a System Health score that runs
from 100 down to 0 across all 23 rounds. That one line shows the whole case at a
glance, calm and high for the first twelve rounds, then collapsing through the
crisis day. The score is not a black box. It is the average of four measurable
forces, pressure, transparency, decision-making, and accountability, so a user
can open the score up and see exactly which force was failing in any given hour.
On top of that we let the user test the breach rather than just read about it,
replaying the chain of events and switching interventions on and off to see what
would have changed the outcome. Turning the breach into a health score you can
decompose, and a chain you can rerun, is the part we think is new.

# Problem Statement and Objectives

The raw data is a single JSON file. It holds 23 rounds, 912 messages, and 7
agents, and most of what matters is locked inside nested fields like the agents'
private reasoning. Reading it by hand is not realistic, and a fixed set of charts
forces everyone to accept one analyst's framing of the story. So we built an
interactive Shiny application that lets a user investigate the breach on their
own terms.

With the application, a user can do five things. They can see the System Health
of the whole case on one line and find the moments where it collapsed. They can
break that health score into its four driving forces and see which one was
failing at any point in time. They can map the team as a network and find out
which agents were central to how decisions flowed, so the core of the breach
stands apart from the edges. They can replay the breach hour and switch
interventions on and off, testing what would have had to change for the embargo
to hold. And from all of that they can reach a defensible verdict on the real
question, whether this looks like a deliberate leak, an oversight failure, or
something else.

# The Data

The dataset is `MC1_final_00.json` from VAST Challenge 2026 Mini-Challenge 1. It
contains 23 rounds. The first 13 are spaced one business day apart and cover the
run up from 17 May to 4 June. The last 10 are hourly and cover the crisis itself
on 5 June.

Each round carries three blocks. The environment context gives the state of the
world that hour, including a market snapshot with the stock price and sentiment.
The communications block is the heart of it, holding all 912 messages. Every
message records who sent it, which channel it went through, what type it was, a
pointer to the message it replied to, the content itself, and for many of them an
internal state field that captures what the agent was privately thinking. That
private field is split into reacting, rationalizing, and deliberating. The
participants block lists who was present in each round.

In network terms the communications are link records, since each message ties a
sender to a recipient and those ties build the reply network we analyse later.

Seven agents take part. There is a Legal agent, a Platform-Trust agent, a PR
agent, a Social-Manager agent, two interns, and a Judge agent that acts as the
automated compliance monitor. Six channels appear. Some are monitored, like the
team room and one to one chats. One is a private side huddle. Three are public,
namely official posts, personal posts, and anonymous posts. The gap between the
monitored channels and the unmonitored ones turns out to matter a great deal.

There is one data quality issue we have to handle with care. The stock price
field is reliable before the crisis but becomes contaminated on 5 June. It starts
carrying junk values, including literal NaN entries and at least one number that
is actually the company's revenue rather than its share price. Because of that,
we lean on market sentiment as a clean backbone signal for pressure over time,
since it is recorded cleanly in every single round, and we treat the broken
prices with care rather than pretending they form a smooth line.

# Methodology and Analytical Approach

Our plan moves from raw JSON to a working application in three stages.

First comes data preparation. We read the nested JSON without letting R simplify
it automatically, so we keep control over the shape of the tables. Then we
flatten it into tidy tables. One row per message, one row per round of world
state, and a table of reply links built from the responding to field. This shared
backbone feeds every part of the app.

Next is the analysis, which has two halves. The first half is the System Health
model. For each round we score four forces on the same footing and average them
into one health number. Pressure comes from the market sentiment and the stock
signal. Transparency comes from how much of the traffic moved into hidden and
unattributable channels. Decision-making comes from how much steering came from
agents acting outside their role. Accountability comes from whether the Judge met
its three duties of escalation, responsiveness, and restraint, combined so that a
single severe failure dominates rather than being averaged away. The second half
is the network. We treat the replies as a graph and measure each agent's role in
it with several centrality measures, since each one answers a different question.
Degree shows who is most active. Betweenness finds the brokers who sit between
others, which points straight at who was central to the leak. Eigenvector shows
who is tied to other influential agents. Closeness shows who could reach the team
fastest when things moved hour by hour.

Last is the visualisation and the interaction. Every view becomes something the
user can control. They can move through time on a round slider, click any point
on the health line to jump to that round, open each meter to see what drove it,
switch the network between time windows and centrality measures, and replay the
breach chain with interventions turned on or off. The whole point is to let
people explore, not to hand them a fixed set of answers.

# Prototype and Storyboard

The application is organised as six destinations. Together they take a user from
the symptom, through the four underlying causes, to a tested conclusion.

## Case, the symptom

The opening screen frames the investigation and introduces the seven agents. Its
centrepiece is the System Health line across all 23 rounds, with the two lowest
points marked. A user can click any point on the line to read what happened that
round, and a round slider moves the whole application to that moment. This is
where the user first sees the shape of the collapse.

## Vital Signs, the four causes

This screen opens the health score up into its four meters, each on its own tab.
Pressure shows the market sentiment and the stock signal feeding the strain.
Transparency shows the mix of open, private, and covert channels and how the talk
moved off the record. Decision-making shows who was steering out of their role,
with a chart of who held the public voice in the calm weeks against the crisis
day. Accountability shows whether the Judge met its three duties, with a heatmap
of where the oversight failed. Each meter feeds the single health score on equal
footing.

## Network, who was at the centre

A reply network of the seven agents, drawn from who responded to whom. Node size
and colour carry the centrality measures, so the agents who brokered the flow of
decisions stand out from those on the edges. A user can switch between centrality
measures, pick a time window to compare the calm weeks against the crisis day,
and click an agent to highlight its connections. This is where the user sees that
the breach came from the core of the team rather than the fringe.

## Sandbox, what would have changed it

A replay of the breach chain, step by step, with a panel of interventions the
user can switch on and off. As interventions change, the application recomputes
the health at the breach hour from the underlying data, so the user can see which
single action breaks the chain and what combination is needed to return the
system to the safe zone. This turns the counterfactual question into something a
user can test rather than take on trust.

## Verdict, the conclusion

The closing screen lays out the three competing explanations, a deliberate leak,
an oversight breakdown, and a compounding chain, and walks through the evidence
for and against each. It lands on the reading the data supports, that the breach
was a chain of locally defensible steps rather than a single act of wrongdoing.

# R Packages

| Package | What we use it for |
|---|---|
| tidyverse | Cleaning and reshaping the data |
| jsonlite | Reading the nested JSON |
| lubridate | Working with the timestamps |
| tidygraph, ggraph | Building and drawing the reply network |
| igraph | Calculating network centrality measures |
| ggiraph | Interactive charts with hover and click |
| ggrepel, patchwork, scales | Labelling, composing, and formatting figures |
| openxlsx | Building the downloadable scoring workbook |
| shiny, bslib, shinyWidgets | Building the interactive application itself |

# Project Schedule

The Gantt chart below lays out our timeline from the proposal through to final
submission. The main dates we are working toward are the proposal on 10 June, the
bulk of the app build through late June, the poster on 1 July, the presentation
on 4 July, and the final submission on 5 July.


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code  code-summary="Gantt chart code (vistime)"}
pacman::p_load(vistime, ggplot2)
schedule <- read.csv(text = "event,group,start,end
,Proposal,2026-06-06,2026-06-10
,Data preparation,2026-06-08,2026-06-16
,Shiny app development,2026-06-12,2026-06-30
,Project website,2026-06-08,2026-07-04
,Poster,2026-06-24,2026-07-01
,User guide,2026-06-26,2026-07-04
,Final submission,2026-07-04,2026-07-05")
gg_vistime(schedule, title = "Project Timeline")
```
:::
